refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote operators must reliably command and receive feedback from equipment. The 0.999 threshold (approximately 8.76 hours downtime per year) is a standard for safety-critical teleoperation systems. Refinement clarifies measurement scope (bidirectional link), evaluation period (customer-defined), and the quantitative target.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculation of cumulative uptime; comparison against 0.999 threshold. Requires instrumentation of network stack and teleoperation gateway to log link health events.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate failover or graceful degradation upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The network infrastructure shall provide redundant communication paths to achieve 0.999 availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log and report link availability metrics for audit and compliance.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication link with high-reliability carrier (e.g., dedicated LTE or fiber); no redundancy.
DP-B: Dual independent communication links (primary + backup) with automatic failover logic; link health monitoring and switchover within [X] ms.
DP-C: Mesh or multi-path network with load balancing and dynamic rerouting; continuous link quality assessment and adaptive routing.
DP-D: Hybrid approach combining dual links (DP-B) with local autonomy fallback; operator can command vehicle to safe state if link lost.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Dual independent communication links (primary + backup) with automatic failover logic; link health monitoring and switchover within [X] ms.

potential_sources: Failover logic couples teleoperation control to network infrastructure; requires tight synchronization between link monitor and command dispatcher.
Backup link selection couples to network topology and carrier availability; may introduce geographic or regulatory constraints.
Switchover latency couples to operator safety procedures; must not exceed safe stopping distance for equipment.
coupling_check.notes: DP-B is decoupled from specific carrier choice (LTE, satellite, private network) and from vehicle control logic. Failover is localized to teleoperation gateway. Acceptable coupling; no redesign needed at this level.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Two independent carriers or network paths are available at the worksite.
Assumption: Switchover latency ≤ [X] ms is achievable and acceptable to Safety Manager.
Risk: If both links fail simultaneously (e.g., power loss at site), system cannot maintain availability. Mitigation: local autonomy fallback (DP-D variant) or UPS.
Risk: Dual-link cost and complexity may exceed budget. Mitigation: phased rollout or customer trade-off analysis.
Risk: Link health monitoring false positives may trigger unnecessary failovers, degrading operator experience. Mitigation: hysteresis and link quality thresholds.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-B to specify: (1) link health check interval and criteria (e.g., heartbeat timeout, packet loss threshold); (2) failover decision logic (e.g., primary link down for >N consecutive checks); (3) switchover latency budget (e.g., <100 ms); (4) fallback behavior if both links fail (e.g., vehicle enters safe hold state). Consider DP-D (hybrid with local autonomy) as risk mitigation if single-link loss is unacceptable.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the dual-link communication architecture, link health monitoring, failover logic, and telemetry/tasking message routing. Interfaces with Vehicle for command execution and with Safety Services for link-loss alerts. Does not own the physical network carriers or the vehicle control stack.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (e.g., move, stop, arm) from remote operator console; routed to Vehicle via Teleoperation module.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, link status) from Vehicle to remote operator console; aggregated and formatted by Teleoperation module.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals link loss or degradation events to Safety Services; triggers failover procedures and operator notifications.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication with primary and backup network carriers; managed by Teleoperation module for link selection, health monitoring, and failover.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic. Dual-link failover, health monitoring, and message routing are core teleoperation concerns. Minor diffusion: link health metrics may be logged by Safety Services or Vehicle for audit purposes.

Safety Services owns safety-critical decision logic (e.g., safe state entry on link loss) but not the link availability mechanism itself. High diffusion: link monitoring and failover are operational concerns, not safety-specific. Safety Services should consume SafetyAlert from Teleoperation, not own the link.

Vehicle owns command execution and local autonomy but not remote link management. Placing link availability logic in Vehicle would couple vehicle control to network infrastructure, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion risk.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but not the appropriate owner of a specific technical mechanism. Assigning to NGHE Solution would defer ownership and create ambiguity. Low containment.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module owns the dual-link architecture, link health monitoring, failover decision logic, and message routing. It consumes Network Link (primary and backup), produces Telemetry and Tasking routing, and signals SafetyAlert to Safety Services on link events. This localizes link availability concerns and avoids diffusion across Vehicle or Safety Services.
containment_risks: Risk: If Teleoperation module is not yet defined or is immature, link availability logic may be deferred or scattered. Mitigation: establish Teleoperation as a first-class module with clear ownership and interface contracts.

Risk: Network Link interface may be owned by a separate Network or Infrastructure module not listed in candidates. Mitigation: clarify whether Network Link is a service provided by external infrastructure or a sub-module of Teleoperation.

Risk: Failover logic may require coordination with Vehicle (e.g., safe state entry) or Safety Services (e.g., operator alert). Mitigation: define clear event-driven interfaces (SafetyAlert) to avoid tight coupling.
Risk: Dual-link management may introduce state complexity (e.g., link state machine, switchover history). Mitigation: encapsulate state within Teleoperation; expose only link status via Telemetry.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module includes: (1) dual-link manager (primary/backup selection and switchover); (2) link health monitor (heartbeat, packet loss detection); (3) failover decision engine (thresholds, hysteresis); (4) message router (Tasking in, Telemetry out). Define internal sub-components if needed (e.g., LinkHealthMonitor, FailoverController) but keep ownership boundary at Teleoperation. Ensure Network Link interface is clearly defined (e.g., API to primary and backup carriers) and SafetyAlert is event-driven (not polled).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (dual-link failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (0.999 availability). The mechanism is sound: redundancy + failover logic is a proven approach for achieving high availability in teleoperation. No logical gaps or contradictions. Assumptions (two independent carriers, acceptable switchover latency) are documented and can be validated with customer and Safety Manager.

diffusion_risks: Failover logic may leak into Vehicle if vehicle must enter safe state on link loss. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface decouples Teleoperation from Vehicle control logic.
Link health metrics may be duplicated across Teleoperation, Safety Services, and Vehicle logs. Mitigation: define single source of truth (Teleoperation) and allow read-only access via Telemetry.
Network Link interface ownership must be clarified: is it a service external to NGHE or a sub-module of Teleoperation? Mitigation: document interface contract and ownership boundary.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the clear owner (0.95 containment score). Dual-link failover is a core teleoperation concern, not a safety or vehicle control concern. Interfaces are well-defined (Tasking in, Telemetry out, SafetyAlert out, Network Link inout). No significant diffusion expected if ownership is enforced.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Link availability can be measured via continuous monitoring of link state transitions and uptime calculation. Requires instrumentation of Teleoperation module (link health events) and network stack (carrier-level metrics). Evaluation period is customer-defined; acceptance criteria are quantitative (≥0.999).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR-NGHE-010 is allocated to Teleoperation module via DP-B. Sub-FRs (link loss detection, failover, redundancy, metrics logging) are allocated to Teleoperation sub-components. Ownership and interfaces are explicit.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-B to specify link health check interval, failover decision thresholds, and switchover latency budget. Coordinate with Safety Manager on acceptable latency.
Define Network Link interface contract: API, message format, carrier selection logic, and fallback behavior if both links fail.
Establish Teleoperation module as a first-class architectural component with clear ownership, sub-components, and interface contracts.
Document assumptions: availability of two independent carriers, acceptable switchover latency, and evaluation period definition.
Plan verification strategy: continuous monitoring, log analysis, and acceptance testing against 0.999 threshold.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (link loss detection, failover, redundancy, metrics logging). DP-B is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module. However, the sub-FRs require further decomposition to define detailed design parameters (e.g., link health check interval, failover thresholds, switchover latency, safe state behavior). Proceeding to the next level will clarify implementation details and reduce risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds (e.g., 100 ms) based on heartbeat timeout or packet loss threshold.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate failover to the backup link within [Y] milliseconds (e.g., 50 ms) of detecting primary link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The network infrastructure shall provide two independent communication paths (primary and backup) with combined availability ≥ 0.999.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link availability metrics (uptime, failover events, link quality) for audit and compliance reporting.

assumptions: Two independent network carriers or paths are available at the worksite (e.g., LTE + satellite, or dual fiber).
Switchover latency ≤ [X] ms is acceptable to the Safety Manager and does not violate safe stopping distance constraints.
Link health can be reliably detected via heartbeat or packet loss metrics; false positive rate is acceptable.
Evaluation period is customer-defined and spans sufficient time to validate 0.999 availability (e.g., 30 days, 90 days).
Both links may fail simultaneously (e.g., power loss); vehicle has local autonomy or safe state fallback.
open_risks: Dual-link cost and complexity may exceed budget; trade-off analysis needed with Project Manager.
Link health monitoring false positives may trigger unnecessary failovers; hysteresis and thresholds must be tuned.
Switchover latency may exceed safe stopping distance for equipment; coordination with Safety Manager required.
Network Link interface ownership and contract must be clarified; external infrastructure dependencies must be documented.
Evaluation period and acceptance criteria must be agreed with customer; 0.999 target may be negotiable.
evidence_needed: Customer agreement on 0.999 availability target and evaluation period.
Safety Manager approval of switchover latency budget and safe state behavior.
Network carrier availability and redundancy options at worksite.
Link health monitoring algorithm and false positive rate analysis.
Failover decision logic and hysteresis parameters (e.g., primary link down for >N consecutive checks before switching).
Verification plan: continuous monitoring, log analysis, and acceptance testing methodology.
Cost and schedule impact of dual-link architecture vs. single-link alternatives.
